title: Plague of Justinian
citation: Harper, K. (2017). *The Fate of Rome*. Princeton.
why: First recorded bubonic plague pandemic. Demonstrated that dense urban trade networks accelerate pandemic spread — a lesson still true today.

title: Black Death
citation: Benedictow, O. (2004). *The Black Death 1346–1353*. Boydell.
why: Reshaped European labor markets, religion, and public-health administration. Originates from trade routes between Central Asia and Crimea — the same kind of nexus modern air travel replicates.

title: Columbian Exchange — smallpox and other diseases
deaths: ~90% indigenous population of the Americas over a century
citation: Crosby, A. (2003). *The Columbian Exchange*. Praeger.
why: Population immunity gaps made the Americas uniquely vulnerable. Modern global travel still creates similar asymmetries, though smaller in scale.

title: First smallpox vaccine
citation: Jenner, E. (1798). *An Inquiry into the Causes and Effects of the Variolae Vaccinae*. Sampson Low.
why: First vaccine ever. Established the principle that controlled exposure to a related pathogen confers protection — the foundation of modern immunology.

title: Broad Street cholera pump — John Snow
citation: Snow, J. (1855). *On the Mode of Communication of Cholera*. Churchill.
why: First data-driven epidemiology. Snow mapped cases to a water pump and removed the handle. The pattern-recognition method still anchors outbreak investigation.

title: Rabies vaccine — Pasteur
deaths: ~59,000 globally per year (modern, pre-vaccination era in developed countries: ~100% fatal untreated)
citation: Pasteur, L. (1885). *Comptes rendus de l'Académie des Sciences*.
why: First vaccine developed in a lab against a human pathogen. Established the principle of attenuated-pathogen vaccines.

title: 1918 influenza pandemic ("Spanish flu")
citation: Taubenberger, J. & Morens, D. (2006). *Emerging Infectious Diseases* 12(1).
why: Most lethal pandemic of the 20th century. Showed a novel strain can kill healthy adults disproportionately. Cities that acted early on layered NPIs (masking, gathering limits) had measurably lower excess deaths.

title: Discovery of penicillin — Fleming
citation: Fleming, A. (1929). *British Journal of Experimental Pathology* 10(3).
why: Started the antibiotic era. Bacterial infections that had been death sentences became curable — but resistance has been building ever since.

title: Last major US smallpox outbreak
citation: CDC *MMWR* archives (1947).
why: 6.4 million New Yorkers vaccinated in under a month. The case study behind modern mass-vaccination logistics.

title: Asian flu pandemic
citation: Dunn, F. (1958). *Public Health Reports* 73(9).
why: First pandemic after antibiotics were widely available. Provided early empirical evidence that indoor crowding and low humidity drive transmission — a finding germane to today's GUV planning.

title: Upper-room UVGI tuberculosis study — Livermore
citation: Riley, R. et al. (1957). *American Review of Respiratory Disease* 75.
why: Demonstrated that upper-room UV fixtures could match the infection-control effect of doubling ventilation. Still the foundational study for modern germicidal UV guidance.

title: Hong Kong flu pandemic
status: Historical (H3N2 still circulates seasonally as A(H3N2))
citation: Cockburn, W. et al. (1969). *Bulletin of the WHO* 41(3).
why: Confirmed the shift-pattern of influenza pandemics: each new subtype replacing the previous one as population immunity builds.

title: Swine flu scare and the national vaccination program
deaths: 1 death attributed to vaccine (Guillain-Barré); outbreak never materialized as predicted
citation: Neustadt, R. & Fineberg, H. (1978). *The Swine Flu Affair*. US Dept of Health.
why: Taught the public-health establishment hard lessons about acting under uncertainty, communications, and the cost of overreach. Cited routinely in pandemic-preparedness doctrine.

title: Smallpox declared eradicated
deaths: ~300 million in the 20th century alone before eradication
citation: WHO (1980). *Declaration of Global Eradication of Smallpox*.
why: First and only human disease eradicated. The only remaining variola stocks are held at two WHO reference labs. The model program for eradication thinking.

title: HIV/AIDS identified
citation: CDC (1981). *MMWR* 30(21). Gallo, R. & Montagnier, L. (1984). *Science* 224.
why: First modern pandemic where stigma drove the response as much as the pathogen. Reshaped blood-supply screening, patient consent, and the patient-advocacy model.

title: BSE / vCJD crisis in the UK
citation: Will, R. et al. (1996). *Lancet* 347(9006).
why: First major crisis of a food-supply-borne prion disease. Showed that BSE control requires traceability through the entire feed-to-food chain.

title: SARS-CoV-1 outbreak
citation: WHO (2003). *WHO Global Conference on SARS*.
why: First severe coronavirus outbreak of the 21st century. Contained within months through case isolation and contact tracing — before mRNA vaccines existed.

title: H1N1 swine flu pandemic
status: Seasonal (H1N1pdm09 now part of seasonal flu vaccines)
why: First pandemic tested under the revised International Health Regulations (2005). Highlighted gaps in real-time data sharing between national surveillance systems.

title: MERS-CoV identified
why: Dromedary camels identified as the reservoir. Demonstrates that zoonotic spillover from livestock is an ongoing, not historical, risk.

title: West Africa Ebola outbreak
citation: WHO (2016). *Ebola Situation Reports*.
why: First Ebola outbreak to escape rural West Africa and reach urban centers in three countries. Drove major investment in PPE stockpiles and infection-control training globally.

title: Corsi-Rosenthal box — DIY air filtration
citation: Corsi, R. & Rosenthal, K. (2020). *Indoor Air* 30(4).
why: Open-source box-fan + MERV-13 design field-measured to deliver CADR comparable to $400 commercial units. The single most accessible indoor-air intervention in the schema.

title: COVID-19 pandemic
deaths: ~7 million confirmed; estimated 18–33 million excess deaths globally
citation: WHO (2020–2023). *COVID-19 Situation Reports*. Zhu, N. et al. (2020). *NEJM* 382(8).
why: Defining global health event of the 21st century. Validated mRNA vaccine platforms in months. Established layered indoor-air interventions (ventilation, filtration, masking) as routine public-health practice.

title: ASHRAE epidemic task force formed
citation: ASHRAE (2020). *Building Readiness Guide for Infectious Disease*.
why: ASHRAE's first standing position that buildings have a measurable role in respiratory infection transmission. The guidance document that anchors every scorecard and GUV engine built today.

title: mRNA vaccines widely deployed
deaths: estimated 14+ million lives saved in first year
citation: Watson, O. et al. (2022). *Lancet Infectious Diseases* 22(9).
why: Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna vaccines validated the mRNA platform in months instead of decades. Reshaped the timeline for future pandemic vaccine response.

title: Mpox (monkeypox) multi-country outbreak
pathogen: Monkeypox virus (clade IIb, then clade Ib)
citation: WHO (2022–2025). *Multi-country outbreak of mpox*.
why: First sustained mpox transmission outside West/Central Africa. Established that smallpox vaccines and antivirals are usable post-exposure for orthopoxviruses. **Still active** in Central Africa as of 2026 with clade Ib.

title: Bivalent COVID boosters and the fall wave
deaths: ~100,000 in the US alone in fall 2022
why: First winter where a population with broad vaccine coverage plus prior infection still saw substantial excess mortality. Reframed the discussion from 'will COVID end' to 'what ongoing burden will we tolerate'.

title: H5N1 avian influenza in US dairy cattle
deaths: ~70+ US cases, mostly mild; 1 death in Louisiana
citation: CDC (2024–2025). *H5N1 Bird Flu: Current Situation*.
why: First mammalian-mammalian transmission of H5N1 in cattle documented at scale. Raw milk identified as a transmission vector. **Ongoing** risk of reassortment with seasonal flu strains.

title: Marburg virus disease — Rwanda
citation: WHO (2024). *Disease Outbreak News: Marburg — Rwanda*.
why: First Marburg outbreak in Rwanda. Contained within weeks — a success case for the WHO outbreak-response network that emerged from COVID.

title: Measles resurgence — multi-country
citation: WHO (2024). *Measles and Rubella Surveillance Data*. CDC (2025). *MMWR* 74(4).
why: Vaccination coverage fell below 95% threshold in multiple jurisdictions post-COVID. R₀ of 12–18 means measles is the leading indicator of community immunity gaps. **Ongoing** in 2026.

title: Marburg and Ebola — concurrent outbreaks in Tanzania and Uganda
citation: WHO (2025). *Disease Outbreak News*.
why: First time two distinct filovirus outbreaks were active simultaneously in adjacent countries. Tested regional coordination mechanisms built after 2014 West Africa Ebola.

title: COVID-19 endemic baseline — wastewater surveillance becomes default
citation: CDC (2025). *National Wastewater Surveillance System — Annual Report*.
why: Wastewater surveillance (started 2020, scaled 2022) is now the leading indicator used in most US jurisdictions. Cases and hospitalizations become confirmation, not detection.

title: H7N9 human cases — China
citation: WHO (2025). *Human Infection with H7N9*.
why: H7N9 has historically carried the highest case-fatality rate of any avian flu subtype. **Ongoing** poultry outbreaks in southern China with sporadic human spillover.

title: Dengue expansion — southern Europe
citation: ECDC (2025). *Autochthonous Dengue in Europe — Annual Report*.
why: Aedes albopictus established across southern Europe; autochthonous dengue now an annual occurrence. Climate-driven vector expansion is now measurable year-over-year.

title: This is now
citation: Live signal: see the surveillance map.
why: Ellis is built on the assumption that indoor-air defense is one of the few interventions where individual action and community organization can move faster than institutional response.
